game_name: King's Quest VI: Heir Today, Gone Tomorrow

design_philosophy.core_loop: Explore interconnected fantasy islands, solve environmental and dialogue-based puzzles, collect items, interact with NPCs to progress toward rescuing Princess Cassima
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Time pressure and moral stakes: protagonist must rescue Cassima from forced marriage to the vizier Abdul Alhazred; player choices affect narrative outcomes
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Embody Prince Alexander on a romantic quest across magical lands inspired by classic fantasy literature (Arabian Nights, Alice in Wonderland, Beauty and the Beast, Classical mythology)
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: The game embraces player agency through optional quests, multiple puzzle solutions, and branching narrative paths with meaningfully different endings. It rejects linear storytelling in favor of exploration-driven discovery. Copy protection mechanic (requiring physical guidebook) enforces ownership and manual reading as part of intended experience.

difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Player can choose between 'short path' (fewer puzzles, less satisfying ending) and 'long path' (more puzzles, more satisfying ending), allowing self-calibration of challenge. Almost half of all quests are optional, enabling flexible difficulty through engagement depth.

progression.upgrade_system: Item collection and inventory management; magical map unlocks travel between islands as new areas become accessible
progression.unlock_mechanism: Magic map acquisition enables inter-island travel; obtaining specific items through puzzle-solving unlocks access to restricted areas (e.g., Cliffs of Logic requires information from included guidebook); optional paths reveal hidden areas
replayability_hooks: Multiple endings based on player choices and quest completion
Optional quests with variable rewards and story outcomes
Different puzzle solutions allowing multiple valid approaches
Hidden areas that can be discovered or missed
Choice between short and long narrative paths with explicit post-game feedback about alternative endings

world_building_techniques: Opening cinematic showing Prince Alexander's motivation and magical vision of Cassima
Thematic island design where each major location parodies classic fantasy literature (Arabian Nights kingdom, Wonderland homage, Classical mythology, Beauty and the Beast setting)
NPC dialogue revealing political conflict (vizier's coup, forced marriage plot)
Environmental storytelling through island aesthetics and hidden lore areas
Physical game component (Guidebook to the Land of the Green Isles) embedded in world-building and puzzle design
Multiple ending variations that reflect player moral choices and engagement level

boss_design.philosophy: Not a boss-centric game; conflict resolution emphasizes wit, negotiation, and puzzle-solving over combat. Abdul Alhazred represents the primary antagonistic force as the vizier orchestrating Cassima's forced marriage, but confrontation is narrative and choice-driven rather than mechanical combat.
notable_bosses: Abdul Alhazred (vizier antagonist; confrontation depends on player choices and path selection)
Death (optional encounter available in hidden areas; optional confrontation)

faction_indicators: Cassima represents the legitimate heir; vizier Abdul Alhazred represents usurping authority
Land of the Green Isles as isolated kingdom vulnerable to internal power struggles
Multiple solutions to puzzles suggest different faction alignments or alliance possibilities
danger_signals: Vizier's coup and forced marriage plot as political destabilization
Shipwreck and isolation of protagonist as survival threat
Magic barriers and forbidden areas suggesting lawless or dangerous territories
Optional Death encounter implying moral hazards and irreversible consequences
environmental_storytelling: Arabian Nights-themed Crown Isle as seat of power and intrigue
Druid tribe hidden areas suggesting alternative power structures
Thematic island design (Wonderland, Classical mythology, Beast's forest) creating distinct cultural zones within single kingdom
Magical map mechanics emphasizing geographic isolation and travel barriers as political/social boundaries
